name: M.S. in Materials Science and Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The program presents a broad interdisciplinary menu built on a clear MTEN core (610 Foundations of Materials Science, 611 Diffusion and Solid-State Kinetics, 612 Thermodynamics of Materials, 613 Characterization) surrounded by extensive electives spanning every materials class: metals/corrosion (CHE 654, ME 626 fracture), polymers (CHE 683/684/781/782, ME 678/679), ceramics/nanomaterials (CHE 619, CHEM 748), electronic/optical materials (ECE 657/659/626), mechanics (ME 620/621/675), and computation (CHE 775 Molecular Simulations, MTEN 631 Data Science, 633 Machine Learning). Coverage is exemplary, but with 40 cross-listed courses, requirementGroups 0, and a few loosely related entries (CE 641 Soils), the required core versus elective breadth is not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study fixing a required MTEN core and organizing the electives into materials-class tracks (metals, polymers, ceramics/nano, electronic, computational) so the large menu is navigable.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is broadly healthy: missingCourseCount 0 and deepestPrereqChain 2, with sensible coded chains (ME 620 to 621; ECE 657 to 659; CHE 624 to 709/710/714/722). One clear inconsistency is that MTEN 611 lists its prerequisite as 'MTSE 602', using a legacy/foreign subject code rather than the program's MTEN prefix, and prerequisites otherwise mix coded graduate courses with prose undergraduate background.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the MTEN 611 prerequisite from the legacy 'MTSE 602' code to the current MTEN subject code and audit for other legacy-code prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data, a real gap for an M.S. catalog page that a curriculum committee and prospective students would expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes (e.g., structure-property-processing analysis, materials characterization, computational/data-driven materials design) mapped to the MTEN core.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan groups were parsed, so with 40 electives available neither the required-core credit count nor the total load is published; an M.S. in MSE is typically about 30 credits and that structure is opaque here.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 30-credit total, how many credits must come from the MTEN core versus electives, and the thesis/project options (MTEN 700B/701B).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific for essentially all 40 courses, with rich technical detail (e.g., CHE 722 additive manufacturing, MTEN 613 characterization theory). However several entries carry parsing artifacts where prerequisite text bled into the description ('Prerequisites or The course...' in CHE 684; 'Pre or The course...' in CHE 781/782) and MTEN 701B lists a cross-department corequisite (CHE 791); there is also no plan of study or credit total.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the truncated prerequisite/description artifacts (CHE 684, 781, 782), correct the MTEN 701B corequisite, and add a plan-of-study table with a credit summary.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The elective set is notably current: additive manufacturing (CHE 722), nanomaterials and nano-characterization (CHE 619, CHEM 748), semiconductor devices and fabrication (ECE 657/659), molecular simulation (CHE 775), and, distinctively, dedicated Data Science (MTEN 631) and Machine Learning (MTEN 633) courses that align the program with the Materials Genome Initiative and integrated computational materials engineering. Gaps are mainly in energy-storage materials and materials sustainability/recycling.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an energy-materials (batteries/photovoltaics) elective and a materials-sustainability/circular-economy elective to complement the strong computational offerings.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific across the metals, polymer, ceramic, electronic, mechanics, and computational electives; only a handful (CHE 684, 781, 782) have leading prerequisite-bleed artifacts, so aggregate quality is strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites mix coded graduate courses with prose undergraduate background and have no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), but the legacy 'MTSE 602' code on MTEN 611 and the 'Pre or'/'Prerequisites or' artifacts on CHE 684/781/782 reduce clarity.

missingTopics.topic: Energy-storage and conversion materials (batteries, fuel cells, photovoltaics)
missingTopics.rationale: Despite strong metals, polymer, and electronic-materials coverage, no dedicated energy-materials course appears, a major contemporary materials subfield.

missingTopics.topic: Materials sustainability, recycling, and life-cycle assessment
missingTopics.rationale: With deep polymer (CHE 781/782) and metals content there is no circular-economy or recycling course, now central to materials practice.
Biomaterials courses (BME 651/672/680) are borrowed in, but there is no soft-matter/hydrogels course integrating with the polymer track.

missingTopics.topic: Electronic-structure and multiscale computational materials (DFT, ICME)
missingTopics.rationale: CHE 775 offers molecular simulation, but no density-functional-theory or integrated multiscale course completes the computational stack.
MTEN 613 covers characterization broadly, but advanced synchrotron, in-situ/operando, and atom-probe methods are not offered as an elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: MTEN 631/633 already position the program well; extending toward inverse/generative design and autonomous experimentation would keep it at the frontier.

emergingTrends.trend: Additive manufacturing of metals and ceramics
emergingTrends.rationale: CHE 722 could expand into metals/ceramics AM and process-microstructure-property linkages that industry increasingly demands.
The ECE/CHEM nanomaterials courses could add graphene/2D and quantum-materials content that is reshaping electronic materials.
The polymer track (CHE 781/782) could incorporate biopolymers, degradable plastics, and recycling to match sustainability demand.
Pairing the data-science courses with autonomous/high-throughput experimentation reflects where materials discovery is heading.

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study: a required MTEN core, a 30-credit total, and electives organized into materials-class tracks.
State program learning outcomes mapped to the MTEN core.
Fix data-quality issues: the MTSE 602 to MTEN prerequisite on MTEN 611 and the prerequisite-bleed artifacts on CHE 684/781/782.
Add energy-materials and materials-sustainability/recycling electives to close the main content gaps.
Build on the strong Data Science/Machine Learning courses toward materials informatics, inverse design, and autonomous experimentation.
